data.title: Elderly man dies in fire at Hong Kong public housing estate
data.description:
A 78-year-old Hongkonger has died after a fire broke out at a public housing estate in Kwai Chung.
Police said they received a report at 12.41pm on Wednesday that a fire had broken out at Cho Yiu Chuen estate’s Kai Him Lau block.
The fire was extinguished quickly, but first responders found the elderly resident and confirmed him dead at the scene.
The building was not evacuated and no other injuries were reported, the force said.
It added that police and the Fire Services Department were investigating the cause of the fire, which had yet to be determined.
According to local media reports, other residents in the area saw smoke emanating from the building, while the elderly man had not been seen for a number of days.
Initial reports suggested that the man had been burning a mosquito repellent incense in his flat when other objects caught fire.

data.title: KPop Demon Hunters leads to record exports of Korean instant noodles in 2025
data.description:
South Korea’s exports of food and agriculture-related products, referred to as “K-food Plus”, hit a record annual high of US$13.62 billion in 2025.
The country’s Agriculture Ministry also reported on Monday that Korean ramyeon or instant noodles posted the largest export gains, surpassing US$1.5 billion.
The surge came amid the runaway success of KPop Demon Hunters, which ended 2025 as the most-watched film ever for the Netflix streaming platform.

data.title: Anti-crash bollards in Hong Kong shopping area necessary but ‘could cause disruption’
data.description:
A HK$5 million (US$641,000) plan to install anti-crash barriers in Hong Kong’s busy Causeway Bay shopping area will enhance pedestrian safety, but their exact location, size and number must be further discussed, with installation possibly worsening crowds, a district councillor has said.
Wan Chai district councillor Peggy Lee Pik-yee agreed on Wednesday with the government’s plan to introduce about 60 safety bollards in the area, but said the move needed to meet residential and commercial needs while strengthening public safety.
She also warned that installation works could cause disruptions to pedestrian flow and asked the government to provide more information on expected crowds and police presence, while urging relevant departments to be mindful of “unnecessary delays”.
Authorities earlier submitted a proposal to install the bollards outside Sogo department store in Causeway Bay and some pedestrian areas as a protective measure against potential vehicle-ramming attacks targeting pedestrians.
“News reports have shown that, for whatever reason, vehicles had made their way onto [the area’s] pavements in the past. Also, some drivers might not have been familiar with the pedestrian zone and entered unwittingly,” Lee said.
“I believe that the government’s fundamental premise in this proposal is the same as ours, that is to ensure pedestrian safety.”
However, Lee said more discussion was needed on the details, including the exact number and placement of the bollards.
“You can see that the bollards will take up significant space and are quite close together. I wonder whether we need so many. We do want to protect pedestrian safety, but we also want to avoid the risk of congestion when there are large crowds,” she said.
“I believe there is room for discussion regarding the location, the size, and the area covered by the bollards.”
Lee noted that the area could be very crowded when there were major events held nearby, such as the Lunar New Year fair at Victoria Park, adding that the management and diversion of traffic flow would be a key focus for her in meetings with the government.
She called on authorities to provide figures for reference, such as the expected crowds on busy days and the level of police deployment, so residents could better understand the issue.
Lee also pointed out that while retractable bollards offered flexibility and would cater to residential and commercial needs, such as the loading and unloading of goods vehicles, projected costs could increase if all were made retractable.
In a paper submitted to Wan Chai District Council for discussion on January 20, police and the highways and transport departments outlined the plan for locations around 555 Hennessy Road with about 60 bollards to be installed.
Officials stressed that in light of the potential “public safety threat”, installing robust, internationally compliant anti-ram structures was an urgent and necessary measure, with the project expected to be completed in the fourth quarter of this year.

data.title: Chinese short-video giant Kuaishou taps global debt market with US dollar, yuan bonds
data.description:
Chinese short-video platform Kuaishou Technology is pitching its debut offshore bond sale to professional investors, joining a wave of domestic tech companies raising funds in the global debt market.
The Beijing-based company, which is offering senior unsecured notes in US dollars and Chinese yuan, said on Wednesday that it planned to use the proceeds from the issuance for general corporate purposes.
The two offerings were separate and not inter-conditional, meaning each could proceed independently of the other, according to a stock exchange filing on Wednesday. Key terms, including size and issue dates, have not been decided.
Ratings agencies S&P Global and Moody’s both assigned a “stable” rating to Kuaishou’s outlook.
Kuaishou is China’s second-largest short-form video platform and the third-largest mobile app in the country by total user time spent, with 416 million daily active users in the quarter ending last September.
Despite fierce competition from rivals such as ByteDance’s Douyin and Tencent Holdings’ WeChat video, Kuaishou’s daily active user numbers continued to grow, and the amount of time users spent on the platform remained steady, according to S&P Global Ratings.

“We see further revenue growth as Kuaishou monetises its high user traffic through advertisements and its growing e-commerce business,” S&P said in a report on Wednesday.
Kuaishou generates revenue mostly through online marketing, live streaming, and other services such as e-commerce and its Kling AI video generator. Kling AI was launched in 2024 and updated last year, when it was described as “the world’s most powerful” video-generating model.
The firm’s total revenue for the first nine months of 2025 was 103.2 billion yuan (USD$15 billion), a 13 per cent year-on-year increase. In December, its AI product reached US$20 million in monthly revenue.
Moody’s expected the rate of revenue growth to be 7 per cent to 10 per cent annually.
Following the announcement, Kuaishou’s Hong Kong listed shares rose 4.1 per cent to HK$81.70, their highest since last October.
Kuaishou is the latest in a wave of Chinese tech and internet companies seeking to raise overseas capital to compete in AI.
Last September, Tencent raised 9 billion yuan through a yuan-denominated dim sum bond issued outside the mainland. It was Tencent’s first bond sale in four years.
Earlier that month, Chinese search engine giant Baidu issued 4.4 billion yuan in dim sum bonds, following a 10 billion yuan sale in March.
Chinese investments in AI are beginning to reap early returns. Kuaishou attributed its 2025 third-quarter revenue growth to its Kling video tool and the use of AI to upgrade marketing products.
“AI capabilities deeply embedded across business scenarios” helped the firm deliver a robust quarter, according to co-founder and CEO Cheng Yixiao.

data.title: Man fined US$2,200 in Malaysia for masking Singapore number plate to buy subsidised fuel
data.description:
The man who was caught on video pumping subsidised RON95 fuel into his Singapore-registered car in Johor was on Wednesday fined 9,000 ringgit (US$2,217) for obstructing the registration number of his vehicle.
Malaysian Long Sa Kow, who is a Singapore permanent resident, was charged under Section 108 (3) (E) of the Road Transport Act 1987 for allegedly exhibiting an altered or tampered vehicle registration number.
The offence carries a fine of between 5,000 ringgit and 20,000 ringgit, a prison term of one to five years or both.
In the video that made its rounds on social media, Long was seen pumping RON95 fuel – which is subsidised only for Malaysians with locally registered vehicles – into a car with a partially obscured licence plate.
The first and last letters of the car’s number plate appear to have been covered.

The 63-year-old, who was clad in a navy blue polo shirt and black cap while wearing a face mask, pleaded guilty at the Kulai Magistrate’s Court before Judge R Salini.
Salini said that Long will be required to serve nine months’ imprisonment if he fails to pay the fine.
On January 3, a video clip of the incident went viral on social media. Police later confirmed on January 6 that Long had surrendered himself.
This story was first published by CNA

data.title: China entrepreneur promises football team players a car each, retracts offer after landmark win
data.description:
An entrepreneur in China has come under fire for promising to gift a car to every member of a city’s football team if they won an important competition, then reneging on her promise when they did.
While many people criticised the woman, the local football association said it would not push the woman to fulfil her promise, saying they understood her financial predicament.
The response was viewed 22 million times on one mainland social media platform alone. It also attracted praise from the state media who described it as “winning the people’s heart”.

The entrepreneur at the centre of the controversy is Tang Lei, chairwoman of the car dealer Xinlantian Group in Yongzhou, Hunan province, central China.
In August, she donated 100,000 yuan (US$14,000) to the city’s football team so they could take part in the Hunan Provincial Football Competition.
At that time, Tang was one of the very few sponsors of the team as most people did not rate their chances of winning.
According to a viral video Tang released on social media, she told the whole team: “If you win the championship, I will give each of you a car as a gift.”
“Thank you, sister,” replied the players, the video showed.

Tang’s promise was widely reported by the local media.
However, after the Yongzhou team beat 13 teams from other Hunan cities and won the championship on December 27, Tang disappeared from public view.
She did not send the cars and she deleted all the videos on her account.
This led some players to express discontent privately.
When officials from the local Culture and Tourism Bureau, which administers the football association, called Tang, she said she was on a business trip and planned to check with the team for a new car donation plan some days later.

She explained that both her company and her home had encountered some difficulties, but she was still willing to sponsor the team with another 100,000 yuan.
On January 3, the local football association issued a statement saying that they will not ask Tang to fulfil her previous car donation promise.
“Support should not be a burden and kindness should not be translated into debts. We oppose any moral kidnaps which are not in line with real situations,” the statement said.
“Protecting and supporting enterprises’ development is also part of our social responsibility,” it added.
State media CCTV chimed in by extolling the Yongzhou football association’s attitude.
“The company’s donation of 100,000 yuan in the beginning is sincere and helped the team through the most challenging stage. Now, the company has problems. The association will not push it to realise its promise.

“It is a considerate gesture to respond to the company’s previous kindness. A one-way donation has turned into two-way support,” CCTV said.
“Yongzhou has won twice: in the football match and in the people’s hearts,” it added.
The incident has split opinion on social media.
“If you do not have the capacity, do not brag. Tang only aimed to boost online traffic and make profits,” one online observer said.
But another person said: “Quite possibly she had a big problem. After all, millions of yuan is not a small amount for any company amid this gloomy macroeconomic background.”

data.title: Chinese risk study finds space solar power stations could accidentally zap satellites
data.description:
As China pushes to take the global lead in building space-based solar power stations, a new study warns that powerful lasers beaming energy back to Earth could pose serious risks to other satellites in the increasingly crowded low-Earth orbit.
If these beams miss their targets – because of tracking errors or system malfunctions – they could strike nearby spacecraft, overheat solar panels or trigger electrical discharges, according to a team from the Beijing Institute of Satellite Environment Engineering.
Such discharges can induce unintended currents, potentially damaging on-board electronics and forcing an emergency shutdown. The risk is greater when the laser beam is more energetic or uses shorter wavelengths, the team reported in the January issue of the Chinese journal High Power Laser and Particle Beams.


“Our findings provide guidance for selecting safer laser parameters and for designing protective measures for satellite solar arrays,” the researchers wrote in the paper.
The idea of harvesting solar energy in space and sending it wirelessly to Earth was first proposed by Czech-born American scientist Peter Glaser in the 1960s. Unlike ground-based solar power, space solar power offers uninterrupted energy – unaffected by weather, nightfall or atmospheric interference.
Early concepts relied on microwave transmission and required massive kilometre-scale structures, making the system too complex and costly at the time. But recent progress in reusable rockets, lightweight materials and precise beam control has renewed global interest.
Today, countries including the United States, Japan, China and European nations are racing to develop the technology. The US is in the lead, aided by a Caltech-led prototype in 2023 that showed beamed power in orbit.
China has laid out ambitious plans of its own. Senior scientists and recent papers suggested a national goal to launch a megawatt-scale demonstration system by 2030, with the aim of commercial deployment by mid-century.
But low-Earth orbit is already home to thousands of satellites – including SpaceX’s Starlink broadband constellation – and more are being launched by companies and governments around the world. With such traffic, the chances increase that stray energy beams could hit something they are not meant to.
To explore how space lasers might affect nearby satellites, the team in Beijing recreated key features of the orbital environment in the lab. They placed a small segment of solar panel inside a vacuum chamber filled with low-density plasma – a thin, electrically charged gas that surrounds satellites in low-Earth orbit.
In space, satellites naturally accumulate a slight negative charge. Because solar panels combine conductive and insulating layers, this charge often builds up unevenly, creating voltage differences that leave certain areas more prone to sudden electrical discharges.
To simulate what might happen if a power-beaming laser went astray, the researchers fired ultra-short laser pulses at the solar panel sample. High-speed cameras and sensors recorded brief flashes of light and spikes of current, showing that electrical discharges had been triggered.


They found that lasers with higher energy and shorter wavelengths were far more likely to produce discharges. These events, though brief, could induce abnormal currents in spacecraft systems, potentially damaging sensitive electronics or activating emergency safety procedures.
The results, the team said, highlighted the need to carefully choose laser settings for power transmission in space – and to design more resilient shielding for satellites that might one day share orbital lanes with large-scale energy stations.

data.title: What’s fuelling South Korea’s running boom? Inside the country’s growing fitness obsession
data.description:
South Korea’s streets, parks and travel itineraries are being redrawn as a nationwide running trend shows no sign of slowing.
Once the preserve of a small group of enthusiasts, running has evolved into a mass hobby, with some 10 million South Koreans now identifying themselves as runners, according to industry and tourism data. That is roughly double the figure from 2015.
Marathons and road races are multiplying across the country, and “run trips” that combine travel and races are emerging as a new form of leisure.
Office worker Yoo Jae-moon, 38, became part of the trend last autumn after a suggestion from a colleague.
“I used to struggle even with walking fast,” Yoo says. “Now I run 5km very slowly after work or on weekends, and just hitting 30 or 40 minutes feels like a big achievement.”
Running has helped him lose weight and feel stronger in his back and shoulders, and he is now considering his first half-marathon this year.

The appeal for many lies in its simplicity and autonomy. After the Covid-19 pandemic pushed people away from crowded indoor gyms, outdoor activities such as hiking and tennis surged – and that same desire for fresh air, flexible schedules and solo workouts has since propelled running into the mainstream.
Compared with other sports, running also requires minimal equipment and can be done almost anywhere, making it especially attractive to busy urban workers.
For 36-year-old Seoul office worker Choi Jae-hee, the mental payoff outweighs the physical strain. Having cycled through ballet, Brazilian jiu-jitsu and Pilates, she now calls running her preferred form of exercise.
“When I run, I just start smiling,” she says. “It gives me way more dopamine than any other cardio and really shifts my mindset in a positive way.” Running outdoors, she adds, lets her “feel the wind, watch people and scenery go by” at her own pace.
Choi completed a city marathon last year and describes the experience as “overwhelming in the best way”. Large-scale races, she says, turn what is usually a solitary activity into a communal celebration.
“Hearing people cheer along the course gives you so much strength,” she says.
She is now aiming for a half-marathon this year, though soaring demand and rising entry fees have made securing a spot harder.
“Even so, I keep running because there’s nothing like it for maintaining a healthy mindset,” she says.

At the more competitive end of the spectrum is 37-year-old runner Shin Chul-woo, who has gradually built his life around the sport. He began with short solo runs during the pandemic and joined a running crew in March 2024. Since then, he has completed one ultra-marathon, four full marathons and one half-marathon.
“Running is really honest,” he says. “If you train hard, your times will improve. This year, I’m planning five more full marathons.”
Koreans’ love for running comes amid a broader focus on long-term health and wellness, often framed as a “slow-ageing” lifestyle that includes regular exercise.
The number of marathon events in South Korea jumped from 19 in 2020 to 254 in 2024, with more than a million participants in total, according to police data. Credit-card data shows spending at running speciality stores in the country has jumped 216 per cent over the past two years, with people in their thirties driving most of the increase.
The culture now spills well beyond urban tracks and riverside paths. Jeju and Busan market their coastal and trail routes as “run trip” destinations, blending natural scenery, local food and community races.
Travel agencies promote overseas packages combining races and sightseeing in Saipan, Vietnam and beyond to attract Koreans chasing finishing lines and beach sunsets.
Social media is thick with runners sharing their GPS maps, pace charts and sweaty selfies, often tracked and gamified through fitness apps and smartwatches.
The boom has also reshaped the retail landscape. Premium foreign brands have rushed into South Korea with flagship stores in trendy districts, while domestic and global retailers are carving out running-focused spaces with coaching, community events and product lines.

Online resale platforms report surging demand, not only for running shoes, but also for caps, socks, windbreakers and other accessories.
The explosive growth has not come without friction. Complaints from residents about noise, crowding and shirtless runners recently led several popular parks in Seoul to post notices limiting large group runs and loud cheering.
The rapid proliferation of marathons, with some streets closed weekend after weekend, has also sparked debate about public inconvenience, safety and the commercialisation of public space.
Still, for runners like Yoo and Choi, the simple act of putting one foot after the other remains deeply personal.
“I just want to protect this healthy mindset,” Choi says. “If I keep running, I feel like other parts of my life will follow.”
“It’s good that you can do just as much as you want, as much as you can,” Yoo says. “I realised if I run slowly, I can run for a long time.”
Read the full story in The Korea Times.

data.title: Coleman Wong beats Pablo Llamas Ruiz to edge closer to Australian Open main draw
data.description:
Coleman Wong Chak-lam defeated Spain’s Pablo Llamas Ruiz 7-6, 6-0 in the second round of the Australian Open qualifying on Wednesday, moving one win away from making the main draw.
The Hong Kong player hit 34 winning shots, served 12 aces and committed just 11 unforced errors at Melbourne Park’s ANZ Arena.
The day after Wong said “you do not often play a tiebreak like this,” after he beat Ugo Blanchet of France in the first round of qualifying, the 21-year-old battled through another tiebreak 9-7 to win the first set in 51 minutes.
Wong, who is ranked 80 places higher than his opponent, stepped up a gear in the second set, disposing of Ruiz 6-0 in only 25 minutes.
Last week, Wong reached the singles quarter-finals and doubles semi-finals at the Bank of China Hong Kong Tennis Open, both personal bests on the ATP Tour.
The 23-year-old Ruiz’s best result at a grand slam was reaching the first round of both the French Open and US Open last year.
Wong beat world No 142 Ugo Blanchet of France 6-3, 7-6 in the first round of qualifying on Tuesday.
Wong will compete for a spot in the main draw against the winner of Sweden’s Elias Ymer and Denmark’s Elmer Moller.

data.title: At least 4 migrants have died in US immigration custody since start of 2026
data.description:
Four migrants died while in custody of US immigration authorities over the first 10 days of ‍2026, according to government press releases, a loss of life that followed record detention deaths last year under US President Donald Trump.
The deaths included two migrants from Honduras, one from Cuba and another from Cambodia, and occurred from January 3-9, according to US Immigration and ⁠Customs Enforcement (ICE).
The Trump administration was aiming to ramp up deportations and has increased the number of migrants in detention.
As of January 7, ICE statistics showed that the agency was detaining 69,000 people. The numbers were expected to rise following a massive ICE funding infusion passed by the US Congress last year.

At least 30 people died in ICE custody ‍in 2025, the highest level in two decades, agency figures showed.
Setareh Ghandehari, advocacy director at Detention Watch Network, called ‍the high number of deaths “truly staggering” and urged the administration to close detention centres.
US Department of Homeland Security spokesperson Tricia McLaughlin said the rate of deaths had remained in step ‌with historic norms as the detention population has climbed. “As bed space has expanded, we have maintained (a) higher standard of care ‍than most prisons that hold US citizens - including providing access to proper medical care,” McLaughlin said.
The Cuban detainee, Geraldo Lunas Campos, 55, died on January 3 in Camp East Montana, a detention site opened by the Trump administration on the grounds of Fort Bliss in Texas. ICE said it was ‌investigating the ⁠death of Lunas, adding that officials said he had become disruptive and placed him in isolation. Officials later found him in distress, and emergency medical technicians pronounced him dead, ICE said.
The two Honduran men - Luis Gustavo Nunez Caceres, 42, and Luis Beltran Yanez–Cruz, 68 - died in area hospitals in Houston and Indio, California, on January 5 ‌and 6, respectively, both following heart-related issues, ICE said.
Parady La, a Cambodian man, 46, died on January 9 following severe drug withdrawal symptoms at the Federal Detention Centre in Philadelphia, ICE said.

The administration ‌began using that space last year, ‌it said. The Trump administration has greatly reduced the number of migrants released from detention on humanitarian grounds, a move critics say has driven some to accept deportation.
In addition to the in-custody deaths, an ICE officer fatally shot a Minnesota mother of three last week, an incident that sparked protests in Minneapolis and cities around the country.
External observers have raised concerns over the level of medical treatment that detainees receive.
A 2024 report from the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU) concluded that up to 95 per cent of deaths in ICE detention could have been prevented with appropriate care.
The report analysed deaths in ICE custody between 2017 and 2021 by studying thousands of pages of documents obtained through public records requests.
Medical experts who reviewed these documents found widespread evidence of incorrect or inappropriate treatment as well as severely delayed medical care.
Additional reporting by Agence France-Presse
